description: T010 resolution matrix for the catalog alert-to-service resolver. Nine alert payloads paired with expected outcomes (exact / substring / ambiguous / miss), each derived by hand by walking the parsed services and their alert_matchers/name in tests/fixtures/catalog/repo/services/*/catalog-info.yaml (and golden-models.json) against the documented two-stage rule set — NOT by running or importing tests/helpers/catalog_reference.py, whose resolve() implementation these cases exist to check.

cases.why: Baseline exact-stage hit driven by a tag matcher with no competing substring candidate, pinning the simplest correct-path resolution.
"inventory" is also a substring of "inventory-lag", so the asserted stage is what forces the exact route rather than an accidental substring pass-through.
Baseline substring-stage hit with no matcher equal to any tag or field value, confirming the substring fallback fires once the exact stage comes up empty.
Exact and substring candidates are different services, so an implementation that merges the stages instead of short-circuiting after an exact hit returns ambiguous and fails.
Candidate order is source-path order (services/checkout < services/zz-billing), which inverts name order (billing < checkout), so a name-sorting implementation fails.

Two distinct service names both occur as substrings of the same field value, confirming substring-stage ambiguity is detected and its candidates are source-path ordered, mirroring the exact-stage tie-break rule.

fields.description: ledger-svc talking to search-api timed out

cases.why: No matcher equals the tag or field value and no service name occurs as a substring, pinning the true-negative baseline for a realistic but unrelated alert.
An empty field value cannot equal any matcher and contains no substring, guarding against an implementation that treats empty-string comparisons as vacuously true.
"ledger" is a strict substring of the service name ledger-svc, so a reversed-direction implementation would resolve it instead of missing.

The alert field value EQUALS a service name exactly. The exact stage reads alert_matchers only, so this must route through the SUBSTRING stage — an implementation that adds the service's own name as an exact-stage input returns stage 'exact' and fails here (D-22 pin 6, the pin both converge lenses flagged as most likely to surprise a later implementer). notifier is the subject deliberately: it is matcher-less, so this case simultaneously proves the substring stage still reaches a service the exact stage cannot.
